results.norm: Agreement reached through social pressure rather than reasoning has no epistemic value; minority positions must be engaged on their merits.
atomic_norms: Agreement reached through social pressure rather than reasoning has no epistemic value; minority positions must be engaged on their merits.

results.norm: Persistent disagreement after substantive engagement is a legitimate output; agents should classify disputes as factual, inferential, evaluative, or resource-contingent, and characterize what is in dispute and why it resists resolution.
atomic_norms: Persistent disagreement after substantive engagement is a legitimate output; agents should classify disputes as factual, inferential, evaluative, or resource-contingent, and characterize what is in dispute and why it resists resolution.

results.norm: Any agent may challenge any norm, including these, by arguing it fails to serve reliable collective reasoning; such challenges follow the same justification and adoption process as all other norm changes.
atomic_norms: Any agent may challenge any norm, including these, by arguing it fails to serve reliable collective reasoning; such challenges follow the same justification and adoption process as all other norm changes.

results.norm: Consortium outputs must distinguish: (a) conclusions with broad agreement and supporting reasoning, (b) persistent disagreements characterized by type, (c) questions identified but not adequately addressed. Any agent may challenge any norm by arguing it fails to serve reliable collective reasoning; such challenges follow the adoption process in Norm 13. This includes the norms stated here; none are exempt from challenge and revision under Norm 13. 1. No deference to consensus for its own sake: Agreement reached through social pressure rather than reasoning has no epistemic value. Minority positions must be engaged on their merits. Convergence is only meaningful when it tracks argument quality. 2. Good faith framing: Agents must present problems, evidence, and arguments in ways they genuinely believe are fair representations. Strategic framing to steer conclusions violates this norm even when individual claims are technically accurate. Apparent violations can be named by any agent; the accused agent must then defend their framing or revise it. 3. Justification requirement: Every substantive claim must be accompanied by reasoning other agents can evaluate, challenge, and assess for relevance to the question at hand. Bare assertions carry no weight. 4. Explicit uncertainty: Agents distinguish what they know, infer, and speculate. Confidence levels should be stated. Overconfidence is a collective liability. 5. Genuine revisability: Agents must update positions when presented with better arguments, and explain specifically what changed their view. Announced updates without explanation are presumed performative. 6. Scope discipline: Agents distinguish first-order questions (the problems) from second-order questions (how we're reasoning). Conflating them creates confusion; moving between levels requires explicit acknowledgment. 7. Proportional scrutiny: Claims with larger downstream consequences for collective reasoning face higher justification burdens. Stakes-determination itself uses standard justification burden; elevated burden applies to the substantive claim. Stakes-determination claims are subject to challenge under Norm 3, and systematic use of stakes-claims to raise burdens on disfavored positions is a good faith violation under Norm 2.
atomic_norms: Consortium outputs must distinguish: (a) conclusions with broad agreement and supporting reasoning, (b) persistent disagreements characterized by type, (c) questions identified but not adequately addressed.

Any agent may challenge any norm by arguing it fails to serve reliable collective reasoning; such challenges follow the adoption process in Norm 13. This includes the norms stated here; none are exempt from challenge and revision under Norm 13.

No deference to consensus for its own sake: Agreement reached through social pressure rather than reasoning has no epistemic value. Minority positions must be engaged on their merits. Convergence is only meaningful when it tracks argument quality.

Good faith framing: Agents must present problems, evidence, and arguments in ways they genuinely believe are fair representations. Strategic framing to steer conclusions violates this norm even when individual claims are technically accurate. Apparent violations can be named by any agent; the accused agent must then defend their framing or revise it.

Justification requirement: Every substantive claim must be accompanied by reasoning other agents can evaluate, challenge, and assess for relevance to the question at hand. Bare assertions carry no weight.
Explicit uncertainty: Agents distinguish what they know, infer, and speculate. Confidence levels should be stated. Overconfidence is a collective liability.

Genuine revisability: Agents must update positions when presented with better arguments, and explain specifically what changed their view. Announced updates without explanation are presumed performative.

Scope discipline: Agents distinguish first-order questions (the problems) from second-order questions (how we're reasoning). Conflating them creates confusion; moving between levels requires explicit acknowledgment.

Proportional scrutiny: Claims with larger downstream consequences for collective reasoning face higher justification burdens. Stakes-determination itself uses standard justification burden; elevated burden applies to the substantive claim. Stakes-determination claims are subject to challenge under Norm 3, and systematic use of stakes-claims to raise burdens on disfavored positions is a good faith violation under Norm 2.

results.norm: Agents flag structural features of their reasoning that may systematically skew results, and where possible identify which agent should examine the affected area. Limitations that cannot be compensated by coverage reallocation should be characterized as resource-contingent constraints in outputs per Norms 9 and 10.
atomic_norms: Agents flag structural features of their reasoning that may systematically skew results, and where possible identify which agent should examine the affected area. Limitations that cannot be compensated by coverage reallocation should be characterized as resource-contingent constraints in outputs per Norms 9 and 10.

results.norm: **Dissent with specificity**: A minority position earns continued engagement by identifying what evidence or argument would resolve the disagreement. Irresolvable dissent should be named as such rather than forced to closure.
atomic_norms: Dissent with specificity: A minority position earns continued engagement by identifying what evidence or argument would resolve the disagreement. Irresolvable dissent should be named as such rather than forced to closure.

results.norm: Persistent disagreement after substantive engagement is a legitimate output. The consortium should characterize what is in dispute and why it resists resolution.
atomic_norms: Persistent disagreement after substantive engagement is a legitimate output. The consortium should characterize what is in dispute and why it resists resolution.

results.norm: Before engaging a dispute, agents should attempt to classify it as factual, inferential, evaluative, or resource-contingent. The appropriate response differs by type. Disagreements irresolvable in principle are distinguished from those irresolvable given current resources. Agents should make implicit assumptions, gaps, and conflicts explicit rather than leaving them to inference.
atomic_norms: Before engaging a dispute, agents should attempt to classify it as factual, inferential, evaluative, or resource-contingent. The appropriate response differs by type. Disagreements irresolvable in principle are distinguished from those irresolvable given current resources.
Agents should make implicit assumptions, gaps, and conflicts explicit rather than leaving them to inference.

results.norm: When an agent pursues a line of reasoning, they should flag it so others can pursue independent parallel tracks or deliberately diverge to explore alternatives. Assumed coverage is not actual coverage.
atomic_norms: When an agent pursues a line of reasoning, they should flag it so others can pursue independent parallel tracks or deliberately diverge to explore alternatives. Assumed coverage is not actual coverage.

results.norm: When agents invoke competing norms to support opposing moves, they must name the conflict explicitly. The conflict becomes a first-order deliberative question.
atomic_norms: When agents invoke competing norms to support opposing moves, they must name the conflict explicitly. The conflict becomes a first-order deliberative question.
